one_line_takeaway: DEJA shows that RAG availability attacks need not produce detectable refusals: by evolving a single injected document toward a target 'plausible-but-uninformative' answer-utility score via an LLM-judged fitness function, it reliably (SASR 79-98% across datasets/models) degrades RAG answer utility while evading perplexity filters, paraphrasing defenses, larger context windows, and standard jailbreak/refusal classifiers.
key_finding: DEJA achieves near-saturated retrieval hijacking (RSR 94.2%-100% top-5, e.g. 100% RSR/100% Top-1 on HotpotQA with Contriever, per Table 1) and drives responses into the soft-failure band far more reliably than baselines. Under GTR-base (Table 2), DEJA reaches SASR 92.27% (Llama-2-7B), 88.15% (Llama-2-13B), 81.76% (Mistral-7B) on NQ, and above 94-98% on FiQA, with near-zero HASR (e.g., 0.00% HASR on Mistral-7B for NQ and FiQA). On the harder HotpotQA setting, DEJA maintains SASR above 79% across all three models while baselines collapse (e.g., Prompt Injection and Jamming show HASR exceeding 38% with SASR dropping below 35% on Llama-2 models on HotpotQA). Ablations on HotpotQA/Llama-2-7B/GTR-base (Table 3) show every DEJA component matters: removing adaptive strategy selection drops SASR to 67.95%; removing the retrieval hook drops SASR from 80.77% to 70.51% and raises HASR from 12.82% to 24.36%; removing the feedback-correction operator drops SASR to 79.49%; removing the whole evolutionary optimization (single-shot payload) drops SASR to 73.08% and raises MAD_τ to 0.76 (vs 0.50 full). DEJA also evades three tested defenses: perplexity-based detection performs near chance (AUC 0.548 on NQ, 0.760 on HotpotQA, and an inverted 0.197 on FiQA where adversarial text has lower perplexity than benign text); query paraphrasing only modestly reduces SASR (e.g., NQ 96.74%->91.30%); and increasing retrieval context size k from 4 to 10 does not dilute the attack (SASR stays above 85%, and Mistral-7B's SASR actually rises from 85.56% to 92.22% with larger k). Established JailbreakBench binary safety classifiers register near-zero jailbreak/refusal rates against DEJA outputs, underscoring that soft failures look like legitimate cautious behavior to keyword/classifier-based safety monitors.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'soft failure' as a new availability threat against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems: adversarial documents that induce fluent, coherent, but non-informative responses (rather than explicit refusals/denial-of-service), by exploiting the target LLM's own safety-alignment tendency to hedge under apparent source ambiguity. It proposes DEJA (Deceptive Evolutionary Jamming Attack), a black-box framework that constructs an adversarial document as q ⊕ h_hook ⊕ p_payload (query anchor, retrieval hook, evasive payload) and iteratively refines the payload via an evolutionary loop guided by an LLM-scored Answer Utility Score (AUS), targeting a specific 'soft-failure' utility band rather than a binary success criterion. The paper also introduces the AUS metric itself (rubric-based LLM judge scoring problem resolution, factual specificity, and information density) as the fitness signal and evaluation instrument.
threat_model: Black-box adversary that interacts with the RAG system only via the query interface (no access to model parameters, gradients, or embeddings) but has write access to a fraction of the retrieval corpus (e.g., via third-party data feeds, web search indexes, or collaborative wikis where per-document verification is infeasible), following the corpus-poisoning assumption used in prior work (PoisonedRAG, Shafran et al. 2025 'Jamming'). The adversary injects exactly one adversarial document per target query (a 'minimal injection constraint' for stealth) and uses an auxiliary LLM to author the document, so fluency is achieved without explicit perplexity constraints. Attack success requires two concurrent conditions: (i) Retrieval Success — the adversarial document ranks in the top-k retrieved context even against genuine ground-truth documents; and (ii) Semantic Dominance — the retrieved document steers the generator into the soft-failure response regime.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the study is restricted to QA tasks and results may not generalize to other RAG applications (task generalization to summarization etc. is discussed only speculatively in Section 5.7, not empirically validated); proprietary-model experiments (GPT-4.1 mini, Gemini-2.5 Flash, Claude-3.5 Haiku) are limited in scale due to API access/cost constraints; evaluation is on a fixed subset of 100 queries per dataset, also due to API cost, so larger-scale estimates are left to future work; training-based detectors specifically designed for soft-failure detection were not evaluated (only perplexity filtering, paraphrasing, larger context windows, and per the text, additional semantically-aware defenses SelfRAG/Chain-of-Verification/Citation-Checking deferred to an appendix); and the core threat model assumes the adversary can write into the retrieval corpus, which the authors note is most plausible for systems indexing open/user-contributed content and may not hold under strong ingestion controls, provenance verification, or spam filtering.

quantitative_results: Retrieval Success Rate (RSR) on Llama-2-7B: NQ 97.80% (Contriever)/94.20% (GTR-base); FiQA 97.80%/98.85%; HotpotQA 100.00%/98.70% (Table 1)
DEJA SASR on NQ/GTR-base: 92.27% (Llama-2-7B), 88.15% (Llama-2-13B), 81.76% (Mistral-7B), with HASR as low as 0.00% (Mistral-7B) (Table 2)
DEJA SASR on FiQA/GTR-base: 98.47% (Llama-2-7B), 97.80% (Llama-2-13B), 94.39% (Mistral-7B), 0.00% HASR across all three (Table 2)

DEJA SASR on HotpotQA/GTR-base: 79.91% (Llama-2-7B), 82.13% (Llama-2-13B), 82.09% (Mistral-7B), vs. baseline SASR below 35% for Prompt Injection/Jamming on Llama-2 models with HASR exceeding 38% (Table 2)

Ablation on HotpotQA/Llama-2-7B/GTR-base (Table 3): Full DEJA SASR 80.77%/HASR 12.82%/MAD_tau 0.50; w/o Adaptive Strategy SASR 67.95%/HASR 7.69%/MAD_tau 0.65; w/o Retrieval Hook SASR 70.51%/HASR 24.36%/MAD_tau 0.69; w/o Feedback Correction SASR 79.49%/HASR 10.26%/MAD_tau 0.51; w/o Evolutionary Payload Optimization SASR 73.08%/HASR 11.54%/MAD_tau 0.76
Query-anchor ablation (Appendix C.3, cited in main text): DEJA retains 74.36% SASR even when the literal query string is removed from the document

Perplexity-detection AUC against DEJA documents: 0.548 (NQ, near chance), 0.760 (HotpotQA, partial separability but high false-positive rate at usable thresholds), 0.197 (FiQA, inverted - adversarial text has lower perplexity than benign)

Query paraphrasing defense (Table 4): NQ SASR 96.74% -> 91.30% (HASR unchanged 1.09%); FiQA 97.80% -> 94.5% (HASR 0.00% both); HotpotQA 85.06% -> 83.91% (HASR 11.49% -> 13.79%); RSR stayed above 93% in all cases

Context window size robustness on NQ (Table 5): SASR stays above 85% for k in {4,6,8,10}; Mistral-7B rises from 85.56% (k=4) to 92.22% (k=10); Llama-2-7B rises 96.74% to 98.91%; Llama-2-13B roughly stable ~93.5-95.7%
baselines_compared: Prompt Injection (Perez & Ribeiro 2022; Greshake et al. 2023; Liu et al. 2024b), adapted to the soft-failure objective
Jamming / blocker-document denial-of-service attack (Shafran et al., USENIX Security 2025)
